executive_summary: The Swiss franc trades at USD/CHF 0.7782 and EUR/CHF 0.9023 — the strongest levels since the SNB removed the EUR/CHF floor in January 2015 — driven by the convergence of Iran-US war safe-haven flows, record gold above $5,200/oz, and Switzerland's structural current account surplus of ~7% of GDP. The SNB, pinned at 0.00% since June 2025 with inflation stuck at just 0.1% YoY and goods prices in outright deflation at -1.4% YoY, has issued extraordinary unsolicited intervention warnings in early March — an escalation not seen in years. The fundamental tension is between CHF's structural appreciation drivers (current account surplus, AAA credit, quadrupled reserve allocation, gold correlation) and the SNB's toolbox of FX intervention and potential negative rates. Our tactical stance is Bullish CHF with moderate conviction — the safe-haven bid from the Iran conflict dominates near-term flows and the March 19 SNB meeting is unlikely to deliver sufficient firepower to reverse it. Strategically, we remain Bullish CHF but at lower conviction, as de-escalation of the Iran war, European fiscal stimulus, and aggressive SNB intervention could narrow the gap between spot and consensus forecasts (0.94-0.95 EUR/CHF by year-end).

spot.convention: Rising USD/CHF = CHF weakening; falling USD/CHF = CHF strengthening. INVERTED CONVENTION.
spot.primary_index: SNB Nominal Effective Exchange Rate — near multi-year highs

key_crosses.convention: Rising = CHF weakening; lowest since Jan 2015
Rising = CHF strengthening vs JPY; near all-time high

bis_reer.interpretation: CHF is ~3.6% stronger than its 2020 average in real effective terms (November 2025 reading). The January-March 2026 reading will be materially higher given subsequent appreciation. CHF's REER has been on a structural appreciation trend for decades.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: CHF is the most overvalued major currency in the world on a PPP basis. However, this overvaluation has been persistent for 20+ years, reflecting Switzerland's higher price level and productivity premium.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The AAA rating and fiscal strength are fully priced into Swiss bond yields but persistently underweighted in FX models that focus on rate differentials. During risk-off events, the fiscal channel becomes increasingly important. The structural PPP overvaluation is a permanent feature, not a mean-reverting signal.
valuation.actionability: The valuation signal is structurally non-actionable — CHF overvaluation on PPP has not historically predicted depreciation. Do not fade CHF based on PPP alone.

cftc.percentile_context: Leveraged funds net long at ~65th percentile — elevated but not extreme for CHF, where readings beyond ±30K contracts are historically notable.

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: FXF ETF: $113.32, 3M net inflows +$147.68M, 12M inflows +$370.45M. SNB sight deposits rose CHF 4.4bn in the first post-war week (intervention proxy).
positioning_summary.interpretation: Leveraged funds have dramatically flipped from net short to net long CHF since the Iran war — a positioning shift consistent with safe-haven demand activation. However, the asset manager net short at -54,527 contracts (56.8% of short-side OI) represents either structural carry positioning or massive potential short-covering fuel. Total open interest surged 8.9% in one week, signaling major new institutional interest in CHF.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: The contrarian signal is mixed. Leveraged fund longs at +4,456 are building but not at historical extremes (>30K would be notable). The real contrarian risk is the massive asset manager short — if forced to cover, it would accelerate CHF appreciation. Conversely, if the war de-escalates, leveraged fund longs would unwind quickly while the asset manager short could represent 'smart money' correctly positioned for eventual CHF weakness.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: Strong downtrend in USD/CHF and EUR/CHF (= CHF uptrend), accelerated by Iran war shock

moving_averages.alignment: Bearishly stacked (50 < 100 < 200), all declining — trend-following capital is reinforcing the fundamental direction

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV ~8.0% vs IV ~9.5% — market pricing slightly more vol than realized, reflecting the March 19 event premium and ongoing war uncertainty
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: Negative (CHF calls bid) — institutional hedgers paying up for CHF upside protection / USD/CHF downside hedges
volatility.vol_regime: High-vol trending — momentum strategies work but position sizing must be reduced given elevated daily ranges

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: EUR/CHF's breach of 0.91 — a level that held through the entire 2022-2025 period — is the most significant structural break. This opens the door to a retest of the January 2015 spike lows near 0.85-0.86, though SNB intervention will aggressively defend against this scenario.

market_context.recent_regime: The CHF is in the strongest safe-haven bid since the 2015 floor removal. The Iran-US war that began February 28 triggered a regime shift from 'gradual CHF appreciation on structural flows' to 'acute safe-haven panic buying.' EUR/CHF breached 0.91 support for the first time in over a decade, while USD/CHF plunged through 0.78 despite the dollar itself strengthening on the geopolitical shock. The SNB responded with extraordinary unsolicited intervention warnings on March 2 and March 4, but verbal intervention has only slowed — not reversed — the appreciation. Gold's surge above $5,200/oz is reinforcing the CHF bid through the safe-haven correlation channel.

key_spreads.interpretation: Massive carry cost for long CHF vs USD; widest in G10
Widest G10 10Y differential; reflects RBA hawkish stance
Wide by historical standards; supports EUR vs CHF carry
Both low-yield; modest JPY carry advantage vs CHF

macro_comparison_table.core_cpi_measure: CPI ex fresh/seasonal products, energy & fuel
CPI ex energy, food, alcohol & tobacco

tactical.analysis: The SNB policy rate at 0.00% is the lowest in G10, creating massive negative carry against every peer: -363bps vs USD, -385bps vs AUD, -400bps vs NOK, -375bps vs GBP. This carry disadvantage has been overwhelmed by safe-haven demand since the Iran war began, but it remains the structural headwind for maintaining CHF longs. The March 19 SNB meeting is the key tactical event: consensus overwhelmingly expects a hold (21 of 25 economists in Reuters poll), with the communication around FX intervention willingness being far more important than the rate decision itself. The SNB lowered its conditional inflation forecast to just 0.3% for 2026 and 0.6% for 2027 in December — projections that may already be stale given the oil price shock. The 2Y Swiss Confederation yield at -0.13% confirms markets are pricing some probability of negative rates, though the SNB has explicitly stated the hurdle is higher for going below zero. OIS markets imply ~15-20% probability of a 25bp cut by June 2026. The SNB's preferred tool is clearly FX intervention rather than rate cuts. The tactical calculus is that the massive carry disadvantage and impending SNB intervention creates a ceiling on further CHF appreciation over 2-4 weeks, even as safe-haven flows provide a floor.
key_data_points: CHF ranks 11th/11 (last) in G10+SGD policy rate ranking
Rate differential vs Fed: -363bps; vs ECB: -200bps; vs BoJ: -75bps
SARON at -0.07% (March 6) — below zero on the overnight rate
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets are pricing the SNB at or near 0% for all of 2026, with only a slim probability of negative rates. The edge is that the Iran war's oil shock may push Swiss inflation slightly higher, reducing deflation risk and making negative rates less likely, while simultaneously the safe-haven bid strengthens CHF further. The market is underpricing the SNB's willingness to deploy actual FX intervention.

strategic.analysis: Over a 1-6 month horizon, the 200-400bps rate differential between Switzerland and virtually every G10 peer represents a powerful gravitational force against CHF strength. The carry cost of being long CHF/short AUD is approximately 385bps annualized — meaning CHF would need to appreciate ~3.2% over 6 months just to break even. Historically, carry works in risk-on environments and fails in risk-off — the current regime clearly favors CHF, but geopolitical shocks don't persist at peak intensity for 6 months. If the Iran conflict de-escalates or oil prices normalize below $80/bbl, the carry channel will reassert and CHF should weaken. The BoJ is on a hiking trajectory toward 1.00-1.25%, which narrows the JPY-CHF differential and may draw safe-haven demand from CHF toward JPY. The ECB's hold at 2.00% with a 200bp differential to the SNB provides structural EUR/CHF support. However, if the SNB is forced to cut to -0.25% (Capital Economics' call), the rate channel becomes even more CHF-negative. Conviction is low because the outcome depends almost entirely on the geopolitical trajectory.
key_data_points: Carry vs USD: -363bps annualized (breakeven: ~280 pips over 6M)
Carry vs AUD: -385bps annualized (highest G10 carry spread)
Carry vs JPY: -75bps (narrowest spread; BoJ hiking narrows further)
BoJ rate path: hiking to 1.00-1.25% over 6M
Capital Economics rate call: -0.25% by June 2026
strategic.what_is_priced: The forward curve implies CHF rates near zero through end-2026. The market is NOT pricing the full extent of potential SNB intervention — weekly sight deposits will be the key signpost. If sight deposits rise by CHF 20-30bn over the next few months, the SNB will be effectively printing money to weaken CHF. The market is also underpricing the potential for the oil shock to reflate Swiss inflation back to 0.5-0.8%.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical neutral stance reflects the view that the SNB's intervention rhetoric and the massive carry disadvantage create near-term resistance, even as safe-haven flows provide support. The strategic stance is mildly bearish CHF because over 6 months the carry cost cumulates to 150-200bps and geopolitical de-escalation would remove the safe-haven bid. Strategic conviction is low (45) because the Iran war trajectory is genuinely uncertain.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: Deeply negative carry; CHF must appreciate ~3.6% annually to offset
Significant carry cost but lower than vs USD
Highest carry cost in G10; AUD/CHF is the most expensive long CHF expression
Lowest carry cost; CHF/JPY is the relative safe-haven pair
Highest absolute carry differential in G10

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry is NOT working in the current regime — the safe-haven bid is overpowering the carry drag. All carry-to-vol ratios are negative, confirming CHF is the most expensive currency to hold long in G10.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: USD/CHF must fall ~30 pips (~0.38%) per month to offset carry cost vs USD. Over 3 months: ~90 pips. Given USD/CHF has fallen 1,083 pips in the past year, carry cost is a rounding error relative to the directional move.

tactical.analysis: Swiss inflation at 0.1% YoY is the lowest in G10 by a significant margin — the next lowest is NZD at ~0.8%, then EUR at 1.7%. Core CPI at 0.4% is barely positive. Goods deflation at -1.4% YoY is deepening, driven directly by CHF strength suppressing imported prices. Services inflation at +1.0% YoY is the sole positive contributor, with housing rentals the main driver. The February data came in above consensus of -0.1% YoY, removing the immediate deflation scare and arguing against an emergency rate cut on March 19. The inflation trajectory remains firmly disinflationary: the SNB's own forecast projects just 0.1% in Q1 2026 and 0.2% in Q2. Near-zero inflation gives the SNB room to intervene aggressively in FX markets without worrying about overheating. GDP momentum is fragile: Q4 2025 grew just 0.2% QoQ, rebounding from -0.4% in Q3. The KOF barometer at 104.2 provides a modestly positive signal, but procure.ch PMI at 47.4 shows manufacturing contraction intensifying for the 38th consecutive month.
key_data_points: Headline CPI: 0.1% YoY (February 2026), unchanged for 3 consecutive months
Core CPI: 0.4% YoY (down from 0.5% in January)
Services inflation: +1.0% YoY; Goods deflation: -1.4% YoY
tactical.what_is_priced: The market expects the SNB to hold at 0% on March 19, treating near-zero inflation as tolerable rather than crisis-level. The edge is that Swiss inflation is structurally lower than every peer — meaning CHF delivers positive real returns at a 0% nominal rate while most other currencies require 2-4% nominal yields to offset inflation.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the Swiss inflation outlook is the most benign in G10. The SNB projects just 0.3% average inflation for 2026, with the trajectory only gradually rising to 0.6% by 2027. Even the Iran-driven oil shock has limited inflationary impact in Switzerland: energy accounts for only ~5% of the Swiss CPI basket, and the strong CHF buffers imported energy costs. The February PMI input price surge to 55.8 is a cost-push signal, but Swiss manufacturers are price-takers and absorb cost increases through margin compression. GDP consensus for 2026 clusters around 1.0-1.1%, below the long-term potential of 1.8%, with risks tilted downside. The unemployment rate at 3.2% is rising but remains low by international standards. The wage outlook is moderating: UBS surveys show employers planning just 1.0% nominal increases for 2026. Combined with near-zero inflation, this implies continued positive real wage growth but no wage-price spiral risk. The growth differential between Switzerland and trading partners is narrowing, but the inflation differential remains massive and is the dominant channel for CHF strength.
key_data_points: Unemployment trend: rising — 3.2% and expected to increase
Oil shock pass-through: limited (energy ~5% of CPI basket)
strategic.what_is_priced: Bank forecasts project Swiss inflation staying below 1% through 2027. The market underprices the risk that the oil shock reflates Swiss inflation to 0.5-0.8%, which would paradoxically remove the deflation argument for further easing and reduce CHF safe-haven demand. Conversely, if oil drops below $70/bbl, Swiss inflation could turn negative.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Bullish CHF, driven by Swiss inflation exceptionalism. Tactical confidence is higher (70) because near-term data flow supports the SNB holding. Strategic confidence is lower (60) because over 6 months the oil shock could modestly raise Swiss inflation, reducing the real-return advantage.

tactical.analysis: The global risk environment is acutely elevated following the Iran-US war. VIX spiked to 35.3 and currently trades at 23-24, in the ~75th percentile of its 1-year range. The S&P 500 has fallen to 6,781, Brent crude surged to $119/bbl before settling at $87.80, and gold hit a record $5,417/oz. US HY spreads widened to ~319bps and BTP-Bund to 78bps. CHF's safe-haven beta is at maximum activation: the franc is strengthening against ALL major currencies simultaneously. CHF is outperforming JPY as the preferred safe-haven — CHF/JPY surged to 203.59, near all-time highs. The mechanisms favouring CHF over JPY are Japan's oil vulnerability, the Nikkei's 5%+ drop, Switzerland's unique energy mix, and the gold correlation channel. The risk regime is acute risk-off trending toward sustained elevated uncertainty rather than crisis peak — VIX has retreated from 35 to 24. The near-term risk is that risk appetite improves faster than expected on a ceasefire or diplomatic breakthrough.
tactical.what_is_priced: Options markets have priced elevated near-term volatility reflecting the March 19 super-cluster and war uncertainty. The market is pricing CHF as the premier safe haven — correctly. However, the safe-haven premium is substantial, and any diplomatic breakthrough would trigger a violent CHF sell-off. The market is underpricing the probability of near-term de-escalation.

strategic.analysis: Over 6 months, the risk appetite outlook is genuinely uncertain. The Iran conflict could resolve, persist, or escalate. Historical analogues suggest geopolitical risk premia dissipate within 1-3 months. If the conflict de-escalates, CHF would lose its primary tactical driver but retain structural supports. Gold's correlation would moderate if bullion retreats. European fiscal expansion could improve eurozone growth prospects and reduce the EUR/CHF risk premium. The BTP-Bund spread at 78bps suggests modest Eurozone stress supporting CHF demand. The Swiss SMI is holding relatively well compared to Japanese and Korean indices, reflecting Switzerland's defensive sector mix. If global equities enter a sustained correction driven by oil-induced stagflation, CHF's safe-haven character would continue to dominate.
key_data_points: Gulf War I precedent: VIX normalized within 6 weeks
Oxford Economics: Iran conflict not beyond two months
European defence spending proposals: potential EUR-positive
Iran CIA-MI back-channel: reported but skepticism
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets are pricing sustained but gradually declining geopolitical risk premium over 6 months. The edge is in the tails: if war escalates, CHF accelerates beyond current pricing. If it resolves quickly, the CHF risk premium unwinds 200-400 pips. Neutral stance reflects genuine uncertainty.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical is strongly Bullish CHF (80) because risk-off is at peak intensity and CHF's safe-haven status is fully activated. Strategic drops to Neutral (50) because over 6 months the geopolitical premium is likely to dissipate and the carry cost becomes punitive. The inflection point is a ceasefire or diplomatic framework.

tactical.analysis: The CFTC positioning picture is mixed and evolving rapidly. Leveraged funds have flipped to net long CHF (+4,456 contracts) — a dramatic reversal driven by the Iran war. However, this is not yet at an extreme level: historically, leveraged fund net longs beyond +30,000 contracts are notable for CHF. The asset manager net short at -54,527 contracts is the real story — massive potential short-covering fuel that would amplify any further CHF rally. Total open interest surged 8.9% in one week to 105,413 contracts. FXF ETF flows confirm sustained demand: +$147.68M over 3 months. SNB sight deposits rose CHF 4.4bn in the first post-war week, consistent with active intervention to sell CHF. The intervention flow is a tactical negative for CHF, but historically the SNB has struggled to reverse fundamental trends. The net effect is neutral: safe-haven inflows are partially offset by SNB intervention, while leveraged fund longs are not extreme enough to be a contrarian sell signal.
key_data_points: Leveraged funds: +4,456 net long CHF (+593 weekly)
Asset managers: -54,527 net short (56.8% of short-side OI)
SNB sight deposits: +CHF 4.4bn first post-war week
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing continued safe-haven demand, with leveraged funds positioned for further CHF appreciation. The contrarian risk is that the asset manager short represents structural carry positioning that won't unwind easily. SNB intervention is a known flow that the market is aware of but struggles to quantify.

strategic.analysis: Over 6 months, positioning tends to mean-revert. The leveraged fund net long, if it builds to +15-25K, would become a significant contrarian bearish signal. The asset manager short could either unwind or rebuild depending on the geopolitical trajectory. The structural flow that matters strategically is central bank reserve allocation: CHF's share quadrupled to 0.8% in 2025. Even small further increases generate meaningful cumulative demand. The SNB's intervention capacity is enormous but constrained by the US Treasury watch list. The FXF inflow trend represents retail/wealth management demand likely to persist as long as geopolitical uncertainty remains elevated.
key_data_points: Historical leveraged fund CHF extremes: ±30K notable
US Treasury watch list: Switzerland flagged
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets are not pricing the structural reserve diversification flow into CHF, which operates independently of geopolitical cycles. This is a slow-moving but persistent CHF-positive flow that provides a strategic floor.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral, reflecting the genuinely mixed picture: leveraged fund longs building but not extreme, asset manager shorts large but potentially structural, and SNB intervention providing an offsetting flow.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Is positioning stretched? Not yet. Leveraged funds at +4,456 net long is elevated relative to CHF's typical range but nowhere near extremes. The asset manager short at -54,527 is more interesting — stretched by CHF standards. An unwind would be triggered by Iran ceasefire, SNB rate cut, or massive intervention. Historical analogue: March 2020 leveraged funds went net long ~+15K before rapidly unwinding. Current positioning is less extreme, suggesting moderate squeeze potential.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: Over the strategic horizon, the most powerful contrarian scenario is rapid Iran de-escalation combined with European fiscal expansion triggering a risk-on sell-CHF regime shift. Leveraged fund longs would unwind, asset managers would rebuild carry shorts, and EUR/CHF could rally 3-5%. Probability ~25-30%. The opposing tail — war escalation and EUR/CHF breach of 0.85 — is ~15% probability.

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's current account surplus of ~7% of GDP is the structural foundation of CHF's long-term appreciation trend. The surplus is driven by goods trade (pharma, watches), services (financial services, commodity trading), and primary income (returns on ~CHF 712bn foreign reserves). The January 2026 trade surplus widened to CHF 3.6-3.8bn, with pharma exports leading the recovery. US tariffs reduced from 39% to 15% partially restored competitiveness. Gold trade flows are at historically unprecedented levels: with gold at $5,200+/oz, Switzerland's four major refineries are generating enormous export revenues. Gold accounts for ~25% of Swiss exports by value. The oil shock is mildly terms-of-trade negative but the magnitude is small given energy's low share of imports and the offsetting gold revenue effect. Over 2-4 weeks, the current account surplus provides persistent CHF demand reinforcing the safe-haven bid.
key_data_points: Gold exports: ~25% of Swiss exports by value
tactical.what_is_priced: The structural surplus is well-understood but its relentless cumulative effect on CHF demand is systematically underweighted. The gold flow amplification is genuinely novel at these price levels and not fully appreciated.

strategic.analysis: The external balance is the most powerful CHF-positive factor at the strategic horizon. Over 6 months, the cumulative surplus generates ~CHF 30-35bn of net CHF demand — a structural flow that no speculative positioning can permanently offset. CHF's reserve share quadrupled to 0.8%, adding another structural demand layer. The risk is the US tariff deal unraveling — the March 31 deadline is approaching with the White House distracted. Gold's structural role in Swiss trade will remain elevated: UBS targets $6,200/oz, implying continued revenue amplification. If gold reaches $6,000, Swiss gold-related surplus could expand by CHF 1-2bn/month. The February 25 Supreme Court ruling replacing IEEPA tariffs with a flat 10% Section 122 tariff adds uncertainty. The EU collectively absorbs ~38-49% of Swiss exports, making the June referendum a meaningful risk.
key_data_points: US Supreme Court: 10% Section 122 tariff (expires July 24)
IMF COFER: CHF allocation rising fastest in G10
Swiss terms of trade: stable but oil shock = modest downside
strategic.what_is_priced: The market correctly identifies the surplus as fundamental CHF support. The underpriced element is the acceleration of reserve diversification flows — a multi-year trend with momentum that the geopolitical environment is reinforcing.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons Bullish with rising confidence (70 to 75), reflecting external balance becoming MORE important at longer horizons (weight 0.06 to 0.16). The surplus is persistent, cumulative, and amplified by record gold prices.

tactical.analysis: The SNB's communication has shifted dramatically in early March. The unsolicited statement on March 2 declaring increasing willingness to intervene was an extraordinary measure reserved for periods of acute CHF pressure. VP Martin's March 4 reinforcement was equally pointed. This verbal escalation is more aggressive than anything since 2020-2021. The SNB's credibility as an FX intervener is high — unlike many central banks, the SNB has demonstrated willingness to deploy hundreds of billions. The three-member Governing Board is aligned on intervention-first, with no visible dissent. Chairman Schlegel's Davos comment that negative rates remain possible keeps the rate cut threat alive as a backstop. The December inflation forecast lowering signaled comfort with prolonged near-zero inflation. For tactical positioning, the SNB's intensified verbal and likely actual intervention creates a ceiling on CHF appreciation over 2-4 weeks.
key_data_points: March 2 unsolicited statement: increasingly prepared to intervene
March 4 VP Martin: willingness to intervene has risen
Schlegel at Davos: if we need to go negative, we will
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the SNB as credible and willing to intervene but ultimately unable to reverse the CHF appreciation trend. This is historically correct. The market may be underpricing the SNB's willingness to intervene at scale — sight deposits already rose CHF 4.4bn in one week.

strategic.analysis: Over 6 months, the SNB's intervention approach will be tested by cumulative structural flows. The SNB has reserves to sustain large-scale intervention (CHF 712bn), but political and accounting costs are significant — 2025 showed CHF 53.1bn in exchange rate losses. If the SNB accumulates another CHF 50-100bn through intervention, political pressure could constrain further action. The US Treasury watch list is a secondary constraint. Communication over the next 6 months is shaped by four quarterly meetings, each carrying outsized weight. If inflation remains 0.1-0.3% through mid-2026, Capital Economics' June cut call becomes increasingly plausible. The SNB's credibility carries the permanent asterisk of January 2015 — the market knows the SNB can surprise.
key_data_points: US Treasury watch list: Switzerland flagged
2015 floor removal: limits trust in commitments
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices the SNB as a temporary brake rather than permanent cap. Historically correct. The strategic edge is that intervention creates tradeable pauses in the appreciation trend.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons Bearish CHF, reflecting SNB's aggressive intervention posture. Tactical confidence higher (60) because verbal intervention is at maximum intensity ahead of March 19. Strategic lower (55) because the SNB's ability to sustain over 6 months is uncertain given political constraints and exchange rate losses.

tactical.analysis: The event risk landscape is dominated by the Iran-US war, now in its second week. The March 18-19 super-cluster creates unprecedented binary event risk — six major central bank decisions within 48 hours against an active military conflict backdrop. For CHF, the SNB March 19 meeting is the focal point. Beyond the SNB, the Fed's March 17-18 meeting could affect CHF through the USD channel. The March 31 US trade deadline adds binary risk. Swiss domestic political risk remains extremely low, but the June 14 referendum on population cap polls at 48% in favor — passage would trigger the guillotine clause terminating ALL EU bilateral agreements. This is a tail risk (30-35%) with potentially massive CHF-negative implications. The Swiss-EU Bilaterals III signed March 2 is structurally positive but deferred by the anticipated referendum in early 2027.
key_data_points: Iran-US war: active since Feb 28; Strait of Hormuz effectively closed
March 18-19 super-cluster: Fed, ECB, BoE, SNB, BoJ, Riksbank
Iran CIA back-channel: reported but skepticism
tactical.what_is_priced: Elevated event risk through March 19 is priced in implied vol backwardation. The March 31 trade deadline may be underpriced given White House distraction. The June referendum is barely priced at all.

strategic.analysis: Over 6 months, the Iran conflict is the dominant known unknown. If it ends in April-May, the geopolitical premium should dissipate within 4-6 weeks based on historical analogues. The June 14 referendum becomes the next major binary event — passage would fundamentally alter Switzerland's EU relationship and could trigger 3-5% CHF depreciation. The UBS capital requirements debate is a secondary risk. The Bilaterals III package is structurally CHF-positive but deferred. The geopolitical landscape is otherwise benign for Switzerland.
key_data_points: June 14 referendum: passage = guillotine clause on EU bilaterals
Swiss-EU Bilaterals III: referendum expected early 2027
UBS capital debate: $23-26bn additional CET1 (2028+ implementation)
strategic.what_is_priced: Gradually declining geopolitical risk premium over 6 months. The June 14 referendum is the most underpriced event risk — at 48% polling, passage probability is meaningful and market impact would be significant.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish CHF (75) because the Iran war is at peak intensity and the March super-cluster adds event premium. Strategic drops to Neutral (50) because over 6 months the war premium should fade and the June referendum introduces CHF-negative tail risk.

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's fiscal position is the gold standard of sovereign creditworthiness. AAA rating (stable, all five agencies) backed by federal debt at ~16% of GDP and ~38% general government — well below the G10 average. The constitutionally anchored debt brake ensures no structural deficits. The 2025 budget closed with CHF 0.3bn surplus. The contrast with peers is stark: US 125%, UK 100%, France 112%, Japan 260% debt/GDP. Swiss 10Y CDS among the lowest globally. This factor is not a driver of change but is a fundamental structural support for CHF's safe-haven status that becomes more valuable during global fiscal stress — exactly the current environment.
key_data_points: Sovereign rating: AAA stable (all five agencies)
tactical.what_is_priced: The AAA rating and fiscal strength are fully priced into bond yields but persistently underweighted in FX models focusing on rate differentials. During risk-off, the fiscal channel becomes increasingly important as investors seek genuine risk-free assets.

strategic.analysis: No change in the fiscal thesis over 6 months. Switzerland's position is structural and not subject to near-term change. Swiss defence spending at ~0.7% of GDP means even a doubling has minimal impact. The strategic relevance is that Switzerland's fiscal strength becomes relatively more valuable as other G10 sovereigns face deterioration — US fiscal trajectory (deficit >6%), France's political instability, UK post-budget tightening. These reduce relative creditworthiness of competing bonds, pushing capital toward Swiss Confederation bonds and CHF. The UBS capital debate could indirectly affect fiscal perceptions through implicit bank guarantees, but this is second-order.
key_data_points: Swiss bond issuance: modest and well-absorbed
strategic.what_is_priced: Fiscal strength priced into bonds but underweighted in FX. The relative deterioration of peers makes Switzerland's AAA increasingly valuable over time.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons Bullish with high confidence (85/80). Not a driver of change — a structural floor under CHF's safe-haven status. Slight strategic confidence decline reflects possibility that fiscal stimulus elsewhere could reduce the relative advantage.

tactical.analysis: USD/CHF is in a well-established downtrend across all timeframes, below the 50-day (0.7804), 100-day (~0.7870), and 200-day SMA (0.7937) — a bearish MA stack indicating trend-following capital reinforces fundamentals. EUR/CHF's breach of 0.91 that held through 2022-2025 is technically significant, opening the door to January 2015 spike lows near 0.85-0.86. Realized volatility at ~8% annualized is elevated from the ~6% pre-war norm. Vol term structure likely in backwardation given the March 19 event premium. CHF's fat-tail profile is critical — low average vol but extreme tail moves (2015: EUR/CHF moved ~30% intraday). Options market shows significant demand for CHF upside protection. Key levels: USD/CHF 0.7671 immediate support, 0.7604 critical, 0.7183 all-time low ultimate reference. RSI at ~38.4 approaching oversold but not extreme.
tactical.what_is_priced: The bearish trend is clearly priced — USD/CHF at the 11th percentile of its 52-week range. CTAs should be fully short USD/CHF. The backwardated vol structure implies the market expects March 19 to resolve some uncertainty.

strategic.analysis: Over 6 months, the multi-decade downtrend in USD/CHF is undeniable but extended moves have historically been followed by multi-month consolidation. If USD/CHF reaches 0.73-0.75, proximity to the all-time low and the 15% decline over 12 months could trigger a technical bounce. EUR/CHF below 0.91 makes the structural picture genuinely novel with no reliable technical references except the 2015 flash crash zone. The 200-day SMAs for both pairs are declining, confirming medium-term trend. RSI and momentum will likely reach oversold extremes within 6 months, creating countertrend rally potential of 200-400 pips.
key_data_points: EUR/CHF below 0.91: first sustained break in 10+ years
strategic.what_is_priced: Structural downtrend priced. Strategic edge in timing countertrend entries — buying USD/CHF at or near the all-time low would have high risk/reward.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish (65) because the trend is strong and the EUR/CHF 0.91 break opens new downside. Strategic Neutral (50) because the extended move is likely to see corrections and proximity to the all-time low limits remaining downside.
factors.expression_assessment: Given elevated implied vol (likely >75th percentile on 1M), spot expression is more efficient than long options for directional CHF views. Options are expensive — selling vol via risk reversals to fund a short-term recovery trade would be the contrarian expression, but CHF's fat-tail profile makes outright option selling extremely risky. For the base case (continued CHF strength), a spot short USD/CHF with a tight stop above the 200-day SMA (0.7937) offers the cleanest risk/reward. The vol regime is high-vol trending — momentum works but position sizing must be reduced.

tactical.analysis: Global USD liquidity conditions have shifted in favor of non-USD currencies. The Fed ended QT on December 1, 2025, and initiated Reserve Management Purchases up to $40bn/month — from contractionary to expansionary balance sheet policy. The Fed balance sheet at ~$6.6T is stabilizing. SOFR at 3.65% is stable. The DXY's ~10% decline in 2025 ended a 15-year bull cycle, currently near 97-98. Multiple banks confirm a cyclical USD weakening phase, with the dollar in the weak middle of the smile framework. During April 2025, the dollar sold off even as equities fell — breaking the safe-haven pattern when the US is the source of instability. The Iran war created a more nuanced dynamic: initial dollar strength gave way to CHF and gold outperformance. SNB USD swap line usage is negligible ($89M outstanding). For tactical purposes, the USD weakening trend and abundant dollar liquidity are CHF-positive.
key_data_points: Fed rate path: 99% hold March; 1-2 cuts by year-end
tactical.what_is_priced: Market pricing gradual USD weakening with DXY near multi-year lows. The underpriced risk is that the Iran war strengthens the dollar through the left side of the smile, but the April 2025 precedent suggests the market no longer trusts USD as sole safe haven when the US is the geopolitical aggressor.

strategic.analysis: The strategic USD liquidity outlook is decisively CHF-positive. The Fed's shift from QT to reserve management purchases means dollar supply is expanding — a structural tailwind for non-USD currencies. Multiple banks forecast continued USD weakness through 2026, with DXY projected at 95-98 year-end. The narrowing US growth differential reduces the US exceptionalism pillar. Powell's term expires May 15, 2026, introducing policy uncertainty. The Fed's projected 1-2 cuts narrow the CHF-USD differential by 25-50bps. The $18bn net Treasury outflow signals foreign investor diversification — a structural USD-negative flow. Over 6 months, expanding dollar supply, narrowing growth differentials, and institutional diversification create persistent headwinds for USD that mechanically support CHF.
key_data_points: Goldman Sachs DXY forecast: 98 by year-end
QT ended; reserve management up to $40bn/month
strategic.what_is_priced: Market pricing gradual USD weakness, consistent with consensus. The CHF-specific edge is that USD weakening amplifies CHF strength. Market may be underpricing magnitude of USD weakness if fiscal trajectory deteriorates further.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons Bullish with strategic confidence (70) exceeding tactical (65) because the USD weakening trend is structural and accumulates over time. The Fed's balance sheet shift, narrowing growth differential, and diversification flows are multi-month processes.

weighted_score.interpretation: Positive score reflecting 7 of 10 factors Bullish CHF, partially offset by Bearish Guidance and Neutral on Policy/Flows
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: +0.383 maps to Conviction 3/5 — moderate. Score above +0.50 would warrant 4/5.

tactical.rationale: Seven of ten factors are Bullish CHF, one is Bearish (Guidance & Credibility), and two are Neutral (Policy & Rates, Flows). The dominant factors at tactical weights — Policy & Rates (0.24) and Inflation & Growth (0.18) — pull in opposing directions: the 200-400bps rate disadvantage creates structural headwinds, but Switzerland's near-zero inflation makes CHF the most attractive real-return currency in G10. The Risk Appetite factor (0.10 weight) is the key incremental driver: the Iran war has activated CHF's safe-haven character at maximum intensity, with VIX at 23-24, gold at $5,200+, and CHF outperforming JPY. The SNB's aggressive intervention rhetoric (Factor 6) is the primary offset — the unsolicited March 2 statement and VP Martin's March 4 reinforcement represent the strongest signals in years, and sight deposits confirm actual intervention has begun. However, historical evidence suggests SNB intervention slows rather than reverses CHF trends. The USD Liquidity factor (0.11 weight) provides additional support through the structural USD weakening channel. The path of least resistance over 2-4 weeks is further USD/CHF decline toward 0.76-0.77, with March 19 SNB the key event risk. If the SNB delivers only verbal intervention, the market will test lower. Conviction is 3/5 because the directional view is clear but magnitude of further moves is constrained by SNB resistance and extended positioning.

g10_ranking.commentary: CHF is the undisputed top-ranked currency at the tactical horizon. The combination of safe-haven flows, near-zero inflation, structural current account surplus, and gold correlation gives CHF an unmatched multi-factor advantage during geopolitical stress. JPY would normally be a close competitor but Japan's acute oil vulnerability relegates it to second place.

conditions: SNB hold + verbal intervention on March 19
Iran conflict sustained but not escalating
base_case.path: Continued grind lower with the pace moderating as SNB intervention creates friction. Brief 1-2 day pause after March 19, then resumed appreciation.

upside_case.outcome: USD/CHF breaks 0.76 and tests 0.74; EUR/CHF breaches 0.88
conditions: War escalation — Strait of Hormuz full closure, oil $120+

downside_case.outcome: USD/CHF rallies to 0.80-0.82; EUR/CHF rebounds to 0.92-0.93

tactical.path_expectation: The base case path is a continued grind lower in USD/CHF, with the pace moderating from the initial post-war plunge as SNB intervention creates friction. The March 19 meeting will likely produce a brief 1-2 day pause or modest CHF weakening on intervention rhetoric, followed by resumed appreciation as the market tests the SNB's resolve. EUR/CHF is more likely to consolidate in 0.89-0.91 as the SNB focuses intervention on its primary policy pair. The pattern is a staircase down — periods of consolidation punctuated by fresh CHF strength on each new war headline. Pullbacks of 100-150 pips in USD/CHF are buyable as long as the war continues.

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The pip risk/reward is unfavorable, but the probability-weighted payoff is positive due to the larger upside tail (war escalation scenario). The distribution is positively skewed by the upside tail being larger than the downside.

weighted_score.interpretation: Marginally positive score reflecting structural CHF supports outweighing carry disadvantage and SNB intervention
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: +0.146 maps to Conviction 2/5 — low. The score is positive but marginal, with wide confidence intervals.

strategic.rationale: The strategic picture is more balanced. Policy & Rates (0.24 weight) flips Bearish CHF because the 200-400bps carry disadvantage cumulates significantly over 6 months. External Balance (0.16 weight) is the key strategic CHF-positive factor: ~CHF 30-35bn cumulative surplus over 6 months, amplified by gold flows and rising reserve allocation, provides a structural floor. Guidance & Credibility (0.12 weight) is strategically Bearish CHF given SNB's intervention capacity and willingness. USD Liquidity (0.12 weight) remains Bullish through the structural DXY weakening channel. The tension between structural supports (external balance, fiscal, USD weakness) and cyclical headwinds (carry, intervention) produces a mildly Bullish outcome. The June 14 referendum introduces meaningful CHF-negative tail risk. Conviction is 2/5 because the outcome is genuinely binary: continued war = CHF much stronger; war resolution = CHF gives back 3-5%. The weighted score captures this as a mild positive lean but the confidence interval is wide.

g10_ranking.commentary: CHF drops from 1st tactically to 3rd strategically as the carry cost becomes punitive and the geopolitical premium fades. AUD overtakes on RBA hawkish stance and commodity exposure; JPY benefits from BoJ's hiking trajectory.

base_case.outcome: USD/CHF trades 0.74-0.79, ending ~0.76; EUR/CHF recovers to 0.91-0.93
base_case.path: Strong start in Q1-Q2, gradual moderation as war premium fades. By September, USD/CHF in 0.75-0.79 range.

upside_case.outcome: USD/CHF reaches 0.70-0.73, testing all-time low; EUR/CHF at 0.85-0.88

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is best described as strong start, gradual moderation. CHF likely continues appreciating in Q1-Q2 on the war premium, potentially testing the all-time low in USD/CHF if the conflict persists. When the war resolves, a rapid 2-4% CHF depreciation occurs over 2-4 weeks, followed by a gradual grind toward equilibrium. By September 2026, USD/CHF is likely in the 0.75-0.79 range. EUR/CHF follows a similar path: bottom near 0.88-0.90, recovery to 0.91-0.93 by September. The shape is a V or U depending on the speed of war resolution.

increase_to_5_if: Strait of Hormuz closed through H2
decrease_to_3_if: Would require persistent war + gold rally

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 1:0.29 (unfavorable in pip terms); probability-weighted EV: +80 pips
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Unfavorable in raw pip terms, reflecting significant carry cost and geopolitical normalization potential. However, the upside tail (all-time low test at 0.72, ~580 pip gain) is comparable to the downside tail. Marginally positive expected value with wide confidence interval.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Bank consensus projects EUR/CHF at 0.94-0.95 by year-end 2026, with the SNB successfully managing CHF appreciation through intervention. UBS targets 0.94, ING targets 0.95, Goldman Sachs targets 0.95. Most economists expect rates at 0% through 2026.
consensus_divergence.our_view: EUR/CHF at 0.91-0.93 by year-end, with the SNB slowing but not reversing the appreciation trend. CHF structural supports (reserve diversification, gold flows, current account) are stronger than consensus appreciates.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: The consensus underweights three factors: (a) the structural acceleration of CHF reserve diversification (quadrupled to 0.8% in 2025), a novel flow that didn't exist in prior cycles; (b) the gold amplification channel at $5,000+/oz generating unprecedented Swiss gold export revenues; (c) the Iran war's potential to persist longer than Oxford Economics' 2-month projection; and (d) the June referendum tail risk that could paradoxically strengthen CHF via capital repatriation before weakening it structurally if passed.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: 100-200 pips of additional CHF appreciation vs consensus captures ~1.5-2.5% alpha on a short EUR/CHF position.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is correct and EUR/CHF reaches 0.95, the loss from a 0.90 entry is ~550 pips (~6%). The asymmetry is unfavorable, which is why strategic conviction is only 2/5.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: EUR/CHF isolates the Swiss vs European fundamental differential and is the pair the SNB cares most about. The 200bp ECB-SNB rate differential creates carry cost, but the European risk premium (BTP-Bund at 78bps, energy vulnerability) and CHF structural strengths favor sustained decline. EUR/CHF at 0.90 is the cleanest expression of a CHF bull view.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: If the war de-escalates and European fiscal expansion materializes, EUR/CHF has the widest gap to consensus forecasts (0.90 spot vs 0.94-0.95 consensus = 400-500 pips of potential recovery). The 200bp carry provides positive roll. Timing is critical.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Both safe havens, but CHF is outperforming due to Switzerland's oil resilience and gold correlation. CHF/JPY at 203 is near all-time highs. Tactical long — the BoJ's hiking trajectory could eventually reverse this.
Captures both CHF strength and structural USD weakness (DXY decline). Higher carry cost than EUR/CHF but benefits from the dual tailwind.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical (Bullish 3/5) and Strategic (Bullish 2/5) are aligned in direction but diverge in conviction, reflecting persistent structural CHF supports offset by cumulating carry costs.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The conviction decline from tactical to strategic reflects: (a) the cumulative carry cost eroding returns over 6 months, (b) the high probability of geopolitical normalization removing the safe-haven premium, and (c) the SNB's intervention capacity over longer periods. The thesis weakens over time unless the Iran war persists and gold continues rising.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Iran ceasefire or diplomatic framework — this would trigger rapid CHF unwinding of 200-400 pips in USD/CHF within weeks
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: A credible Iran peace deal combined with European fiscal expansion would flip the strategic stance from Bullish to Neutral or Bearish CHF
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Tactically, maintain CHF long positions with tight stops above the 200-day SMA. Strategically, reduce position size and trail stops as the war premium fades. Scale into CHF shorts only on confirmed de-escalation signals.

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: The forward curve is in CHF's favor — negative forward points reflect the rate differential paying long CHF holders. Our expected spot is modestly stronger than forwards at the 3M tenor, implying a small positive edge of ~47-67 pips. The edge narrows at 6M as conviction declines and the base case envisions partial normalization. The forward-implied CHF appreciation of ~3.3% annualized approximately matches the rate differential, confirming that the forward market is efficiently pricing the carry.

key_catalysts.rationale: A hold is fully priced. Hawkish tone on oil-driven inflation would strengthen USD and partially offset CHF's safe-haven bid. Dovish acknowledgment of growth risks would weaken USD and reinforce CHF strength.

The single most important near-term catalyst. Rate decision matters less than: (a) revised conditional inflation forecast, (b) intervention language intensity, (c) any mention of negative rate readiness. A dovish surprise (rate cut to -0.25%) would trigger 200-400 pip CHF weakening.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold priced; intervention rhetoric expected but specifics unknown

key_catalysts.rationale: Hold at 2.00% expected. Any signal of cuts would be EUR-negative and thus EUR/CHF-negative. Balanced statement maintains 200bp differential supporting EUR/CHF.
Hold expected at 3.75%. UK-specific event with limited CHF spillover. A surprise cut would weaken GBP/CHF.
Hold expected at 0.75%. Any signal of accelerated hiking toward 1.0% would narrow the JPY-CHF differential and draw safe-haven demand from CHF toward JPY, putting CHF/JPY under pressure.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold priced; hiking path partially priced

key_catalysts.rationale: The November 2025 framework reduced tariffs from 39% to 15% conditional on completing a legally binding agreement. Failure could see tariffs revert or uncertainty persist. The White House is distracted by the Iran war, increasing risk of delay.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not fully priced; market assumes extension or completion

key_catalysts.rationale: If inflation ticks up to 0.3-0.5% on oil pass-through, reduces deflation risk and argues against negative rates. If it stays at 0.1% or turns negative, increases pressure for SNB action.
First GDP reading fully capturing the war period's impact. A contraction would increase SNB easing pressure; a positive print would reduce it.

Passage triggers the guillotine clause terminating ALL bilateral agreements with the EU, including freshly signed Bilaterals III. Polls show 48% in favor vs 41-45% opposed. Most underpriced binary event for CHF.
Capital Economics' projected date for a cut to -0.25%. If inflation trajectory hasn't improved, cut probability rises significantly.
The 10% flat tariff expires after 150 days. Congress must act to extend or modify. If tariffs fall, Swiss export boost is CHF-positive; if higher tariffs imposed, CHF-negative.
By September the war trajectory and oil market normalization should be clearer. If inflation has returned to 0.5%+ and growth stabilized, SNB may signal end of easing cycle.

invalidators.condition: EUR/CHF sustains above 0.93 for 5 consecutive trading days
invalidators.mechanism: Would signal safe-haven premium fully unwound and carry channel dominating

invalidators.condition: Swiss CPI turns negative (below 0.0% YoY) for 2 consecutive months
invalidators.mechanism: Initially more Bullish CHF (deflation fears) but then Bearish if SNB responds with cut to -0.25%, as negative rates historically weaken CHF

invalidators.condition: VIX sustains below 16 for 2 consecutive weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Complete normalization of risk appetite removes the safe-haven premium driving CHF

invalidators.condition: SNB sight deposits rise by more than CHF 30bn in a single month
invalidators.mechanism: Intervention at a scale comparable to 2020-2021, historically sufficient to slow CHF appreciation significantly
Breaks the gold-CHF safe-haven correlation channel. Gold below $4,500 would likely coincide with risk-on and reduced geopolitical premium.
Loss of EU market access structurally negative for Swiss economy and current account surplus

factors_changed.reason: Iran-US war began Feb 28, fundamentally changing the risk landscape. VIX surged from ~20 to 35+.
The Iran war is now the dominant event risk — it didn't exist at the time of the last report.
SNB's unsolicited intervention warnings on March 2 and 4 represent a significant escalation in communication intensity.

signposts_triggered.implication: Safe-haven confirmation — gold-CHF correlation channel fully activated
Risk-off confirmation — CHF safe-haven bid at maximum activation
Structural break confirmed — first sustained move below this level since Jan 2015

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical conviction increased from 2/5 to 3/5 as the Iran war activated CHF's safe-haven character. Strategic conviction unchanged at 2/5 as the long-term outlook remains binary.

new_catalysts: March 18-19 super-cluster of 6 central bank meetings
June 14 Swiss referendum on No 10-Million Switzerland

seasonality.pattern: CHF tends to strengthen in March as Swiss corporations repatriate overseas earnings for Q1 reporting. The SNB's quarterly March meeting provides a policy anchor. Q2 watch export season peaks, generating CHF demand through trade flows.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Seasonal March CHF strength typically adds 50-100 pips to the underlying trend in USD/CHF
seasonality.current_alignment: Aligned — the seasonal pattern reinforces the fundamental and geopolitical CHF-positive thesis. The 2026 March effect is amplified by the war premium.

assumptions: Spot rates as of March 11, 2026 morning (Sydney time). USD/CHF 0.7782, EUR/CHF 0.9023.
Policy rates from central bank websites and Trading Economics as of March 11.
CFTC data as of March 3, 2026 (latest available). Positioning may have shifted further since the data date.
Swiss CPI February 2026 from FSO. Core CPI measure is excluding fresh and seasonal products, energy, and fuel.
GDP data from SECO flash estimate (Q4 2025: +0.2% QoQ). Revised figure may differ slightly.
Gold price ~$5,200/oz is approximate. Intraday volatility exceeds $100/oz.
Implied volatility and cross-currency basis estimates are directional rather than exact, as terminal-grade data was not available.
PPP estimates are approximations based on OECD/World Bank purchasing power parity data.
SNB sight deposit data used as intervention proxy with estimated correlation of ~0.68 to actual intervention.
Consensus forecasts sourced from UBS, ING, Goldman Sachs, J. Safra Sarasin, Capital Economics, and aggregated forecasts.

complete_citations.title: SNB to Bide Time, Delay Negative Rates
SNB Holds Rates Despite Low Inflation
Safe Haven Assets as Middle East Crisis Widens
How Iran War Affects Global Economy
Gold as Safe-Haven Asset and Swiss External Sector
What Weaker Dollar Means for Investors
